questions.question:
The proposition p ^ (~p v q) is:
A. a tautology
B. logically equivalent to p ^ q
C. logically equivalent to p ^ ~q
D. a contradiction
E. none of the above
questions.answer: B. logically equivalent to p ^ q
questions.to_find: The logical equivalence of p ^ (~p v q)

understand.plain: Apply the distributive law to simplify p ^ (~p v q). Distributing p over the disjunction gives (p ^ ~p) v (p ^ q). Since p ^ ~p is always False, the whole expression reduces to p ^ q. So the proposition is logically equivalent to p ^ q.

keywords.explain: p ^ (q v r) == (p ^ q) v (p ^ r); used to split the conjunction over the disjunction.
A formula that is always False; p ^ ~p is the classic contradiction.
keywords.example: p ^ ~p = F for all truth values of p

keywords.explain: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth tables.
keywords.example: p ^ (~p v q) and p ^ q have the same truth table

understand.visual_alt: A truth table showing p, q, ~p v q, and p ^ (~p v q) all matching p ^ q column-by-column.

given.aim: Determine which option correctly describes the proposition p ^ (~p v q).

terms.connects: appears as the outer conjunct and inside the negation
appears inside the disjunction ~p v q

terms.meaning: Disjunction of negated p with q; equivalent to p => q by material implication
terms.example: when p=T, q=F: ~p v q = F v F = F
terms.connects: is the right operand of the outer conjunction

given.plan: Apply the distributive law p ^ (A v B) = (p ^ A) v (p ^ B), then use the contradiction law p ^ ~p = F and simplify.

steps.title: Apply distributive law
steps.formula_raw: A ^ (B v C) == (A ^ B) v (A ^ C)
steps.note: Distribute p over the disjunction ~p v q

steps.title: Simplify contradiction
steps.apply: (p ^ ~p) v (p ^ q) = F v (p ^ q) = p ^ q
steps.note: p ^ ~p is always False, and F v X = X for any formula X

solution.result: p ^ (~p v q) is logically equivalent to p ^ q

questions.formula_note: The distributive law is the key step — it lets us separate p ^ ~p (a known contradiction) from p ^ q, leaving only the latter.

questions.question:
Let p, q be propositions. Using only the Truth Table, decide whether

p does not imply p => q

is True or False.
questions.to_find: Whether the statement 'p does not logically imply p => q' is True or False

understand.plain: Logical implication (entailment) X |= Y means: in every row where X is True, Y is also True. We check whether 'p |= (p => q)' holds. If there is even one assignment making p True but p => q False, then p does NOT imply p => q. Take p = True, q = False: then p = True but p => q = False. So p does not entail p => q, making the given statement True.

keywords.explain: X |= Y means Y is true in every interpretation where X is true.
keywords.example: p does NOT entail p => q because p=T, q=F gives p=T but p=>q=F

keywords.explain: A single assignment that makes the premise true and conclusion false, refuting entailment.
keywords.example: p=T, q=F: p is True but p=>q is False

keywords.explain: p => q is False only when p is True and q is False.

understand.visual_alt: A two-row truth table section showing p=T, q=F where p is True but p => q is False, providing the counterexample.

given.aim: Decide if p does not logically imply p => q.

terms.meaning: Propositional variable acting as the premise
terms.connects: the formula we test as the antecedent of entailment

terms.meaning: Material implication; False only when p is True and q is False
terms.example: p=T, q=F gives p => q = F
terms.connects: the formula we test as the consequent of entailment

terms.meaning: X |= Y holds iff every row with X True also has Y True
terms.example: p |= p^q fails because p=T, q=F gives p^q=F
terms.connects: the relation being tested between p and p => q

given.plan: Construct a truth table for p and p => q, and look for a row where p = True but p => q = False. One such row disproves entailment.

steps.title: Build truth table for p and p => q
steps.formula_raw: p => q is False only when p=True and q=False
steps.apply: Row: p=T, q=T => p=>q=T. Row: p=T, q=F => p=>q=F. Row: p=F, q=T => p=>q=T. Row: p=F, q=F => p=>q=T.
steps.note: We need a row where p=T and p=>q=F

steps.title: Find the counterexample
steps.formula_raw: X |= Y fails if there exists an assignment with X=T and Y=F
steps.apply: Row p=T, q=F: p is True but p=>q is False. This is a valid counterexample.
steps.note: Since such a row exists, p does NOT logically imply p => q, so the statement is True.

solution.result: True — p does not logically imply p => q

questions.formula_note: The truth rule for material implication is the key: p => q is False precisely when p=T and q=F, which is the counterexample that defeats the entailment.

questions.question:
Obtain the principal (canonical) conjunctive normal form of the principal formula

(p ^ q) v (~q ^ r)

where ^ is logical and, v is inclusive or, and ~ is negation.
questions.answer: The PCNF of (p ^ q) v (~q ^ r) is (p v ~q) ^ (p v r) ^ (q v ~q v r) ^ (p v q v r). Equivalently, using maxterms: M_{0,2,6} = (p v q v r) ^ (p v ~q v r) ^ (~p v q v r).
questions.to_find: Principal Conjunctive Normal Form (PCNF) of (p ^ q) v (~q ^ r)

understand.plain: The PCNF (also called the canonical CNF or product of maxterms) is obtained by finding all rows of the truth table where the formula is False and writing the maxterm for each such row. A maxterm for a row is a clause where each variable appears un-negated if it is 0 in that row and negated if it is 1. The PCNF is the AND of all such maxterms.

keywords.explain: A disjunction (OR) of all variables or their negations; represents a row where the formula is False. Variable appears plain if its value is 0 and negated if its value is 1.
keywords.example: Row p=0,q=0,r=0 gives maxterm (p v q v r)

keywords.explain: The AND of all maxterms corresponding to rows where the formula evaluates to False.
keywords.example: A formula false in 3 rows has PCNF = product of 3 maxterms

keywords.explain: Evaluate the formula for all 2^n assignments, identify False rows, extract maxterms from each.
keywords.example: For 3 variables, build 8 rows and pick False ones

understand.visual_alt: An 8-row truth table for p, q, r showing the formula (p^q)v(~q^r) evaluated at each row, with False rows highlighted as the source of maxterms.

given.aim: Find the PCNF (canonical CNF) of (p ^ q) v (~q ^ r) over variables p, q, r.

terms.meaning: Three propositional variables; each is True (1) or False (0)
terms.connects: the three variables in the formula and in each maxterm

terms.meaning: First disjunct; True only when both p and q are True
terms.connects: one of the two disjuncts in the formula

terms.meaning: Second disjunct; True only when q is False and r is True
terms.connects: the second disjunct; key for rows where q=0 and r=1

given.plan: Evaluate (p^q) v (~q^r) for all 8 combinations of (p,q,r). For each row where the formula is False, write the maxterm. The PCNF is the AND of those maxterms.

steps.title: Build the truth table
steps.note: (p^q) needs both p=1,q=1; (~q^r) needs q=0,r=1. Any row with p=0 and (q=1 or r=0) gives False.

steps.title: Write maxterms for False rows and form PCNF
steps.formula_raw: PCNF = AND of maxterms over 0-rows; in a 0-row a variable appears plain if its value is 0 and negated if its value is 1
steps.apply: Row 000: (p v q v r). Row 010: (p v ~q v r). Row 100: (~p v q v r). Row 110: (~p v ~q v r). PCNF = (p v q v r) ^ (p v ~q v r) ^ (~p v q v r) ^ (~p v ~q v r).
steps.note: Four False rows yield four maxterms. This is the canonical CNF of the formula.

solution.result: PCNF = (p v q v r) ^ (p v ~q v r) ^ (~p v q v r) ^ (~p v ~q v r)

questions.formula_note: The PCNF is built from maxterms of False rows: in each False row, a 0-valued variable appears un-negated and a 1-valued variable appears negated in the maxterm clause.

questions.question:
If the proposition p => q is true, then the truth value of the proposition ~p v (p => ~q), where ~ is negation, v is inclusive OR and => is implication, is

A. True
B. Multiple Values
C. False
D. Cannot be determined
questions.to_find: Truth value of ~p v (p => ~q) given that p => q is True

understand.plain: We know p => q is True, so either p is False, or both p and q are True. We then evaluate ~p v (p => ~q) under both cases. When p is False: ~p is True so the whole formula is True. When p is True (and q must be True from p=>q=T): p => ~q = T => F = False, so ~p v (p=>~q) = F v F = False. Since the formula is True in one case and False in another, its value cannot be determined from p => q alone.

keywords.explain: When a given condition (p=>q=T) allows multiple truth-value distributions (p=F or p=T,q=T), we check each.
A truth value 'cannot be determined' when different valid assignments satisfying the premise yield different values for the formula.
keywords.example: Here the formula is T when p=F and F when p=T (with q=T)

keywords.explain: p => ~q is False only when p=T and ~q=F, i.e. p=T and q=T.

understand.visual_alt: A truth table showing only the rows where p => q is True, with the formula ~p v (p => ~q) taking value True for p=False rows and False for the p=True, q=True row.

given.aim: Determine the truth value of ~p v (p => ~q) given p => q is True.

terms.meaning: The given constraint; rules out only the row p=T, q=F
terms.connects: limits which rows of the truth table we consider

terms.meaning: Negation of p; True when p is False
terms.example: p=F => ~p=T, making the whole disjunction True regardless of the other part
terms.connects: first disjunct; short-circuits to True when p=F

terms.meaning: Implication from p to ~q; False only when p=T and q=T
terms.connects: second disjunct; combined with ~p to give the overall formula

given.plan: Enumerate all assignments satisfying p => q = True, compute ~p v (p => ~q) for each, and check whether the result is uniform.

steps.title: Identify valid assignments (p => q = True)
steps.formula_raw: p => q is False only when p=True and q=False
steps.apply: Valid rows: (p=F,q=F), (p=F,q=T), (p=T,q=T). The row (p=T,q=F) is excluded.
steps.note: Three assignments satisfy the given condition.

steps.title: Evaluate ~p v (p => ~q) for each valid row
steps.formula_raw: p => ~q is False only when p=True and ~q=False, i.e. p=T and q=T
steps.apply: (p=F,q=F): ~p=T => result T. (p=F,q=T): ~p=T => result T. (p=T,q=T): ~p=F, p=>~q=T=>F=F, so F v F = F.
steps.note: For p=T,q=T the formula is False; for p=F it is True. Values differ.

solution.result: Cannot be determined — the formula is True when p is False but False when p=True and q=True

questions.formula_note: The implication truth rule is central: p => ~q is False precisely when p=T and q=T, which is a valid scenario under the given constraint p=>q=T, causing the formula to yield different values.

questions.question:
Which of the following is NOT True? (Read ^ as AND, v as OR, ~ as NOT, -> as one way implication and <-> as two way implication)

A. ((x -> y) ^ z) -> y
B. ((x -> y) ^ (~x -> y)) -> y
C. (x v y) -> (~x -> y)
D. ((x v y) <-> (~x -> ~y))
questions.to_find: Which option is NOT a tautology (i.e., has at least one False row)

understand.plain: To show a formula is NOT True (not a tautology), we only need one assignment that makes it False. For option D, check x=True, y=False: x v y = T, ~x -> ~y = F -> T = T. So T <-> T = T. Try x=False, y=False: x v y = F, ~x -> ~y = T -> T = T. F <-> T = F. This makes D False, so D is not a tautology. Options A, B, C can be verified as tautologies.

keywords.explain: A formula that is True under every assignment of truth values.
A single assignment that makes a formula False, proving it is not a tautology.
keywords.example: x=F, y=F makes (x v y) <-> (~x -> ~y) = F <-> T = F

keywords.explain: P <-> Q is True exactly when P and Q have the same truth value.

understand.visual_alt: A small truth table testing each option at critical assignments, with option D highlighted as producing False at x=F, y=F.

given.aim: Identify which of the four formulas is NOT a tautology.

terms.meaning: Propositional variables taking True or False values
terms.example: x=F, y=F is the critical counterexample row for option D
terms.connects: the variables in all four options

terms.meaning: Inclusive disjunction; True when at least one of x, y is True
terms.example: x=F, y=F => x v y = F
terms.connects: left side of the biconditional in option D

terms.meaning: Implication from ~x to ~y; False only when ~x=T and ~y=F, i.e. x=F and y=T
terms.connects: right side of the biconditional in option D

given.plan: Check each option for the assignment x=F, y=F (or x=F, y=T). A formula producing False at any assignment is NOT a tautology.

steps.title: Test options A, B, C to confirm they are tautologies
steps.formula_raw: A formula is a tautology iff it is true under every assignment
steps.apply: A: ((x->y)^z)->y. If z=T and x->y=T, then y must be T; if z=F the antecedent is F so result is T. Verify: z=T, x=T, y=F makes (F^T)=F->F=T. All rows T — A is a tautology. B: ((x->y)^(~x->y))->y. x->y and ~x->y both true means y=T (by cases). Result T — B is a tautology. C: (x v y)->(~x->y). ~x->y is False only when ~x=T and y=F, i.e. x=F, y=F. But then x v y = F making antecedent F, so implication T. — C is tautology.
steps.note: All three are tautologies by case analysis.

steps.title: Find a counterexample for option D
steps.formula_raw: P <-> Q is True iff P and Q have the same truth value
steps.apply: Try x=F, y=F: x v y = F v F = F. ~x -> ~y = T -> T = T. D becomes F <-> T = False.
steps.note: This single False row proves D is NOT a tautology.

solution.result: D. ((x v y) <-> (~x -> ~y)) is NOT True — it fails at x=False, y=False

questions.formula_note: The biconditional truth rule is decisive: (x v y) and (~x -> ~y) have different truth values when x=F and y=F, making their biconditional False and disqualifying D as a tautology.
